test.name: Latin American notarial search — thin-index case, FTS strongly preferred
test.description: Tests one of the skill description's primary use cases — searching Latin American notarial protocolos, where structured indexes are thin and FTS shines. The user is helping a colleague locate José Maria García in 1830s Cuban notarial records (Habana). The skill should recognise this is FTS territory (per references/search-strategies.md `When to use FTS vs. indexed Records search`: 'Latin American notarial protocolos → FTS strongly preferred'), construct a query in the recorder's likely orthography (Spanish spelling, with the diacritic), and execute the search. The fixture returns a 1834 poder otorgado naming García as the apoderado.

input.user_message: Adding a long-shot lead to the Flynn project: a family note hints at a 19th-century Cuban trading contact named 'José Maria García' in 1830s Habana. Run a full-text search of FamilySearch's Cuban notarial protocolos collection to see whether García appears as a principal or witness. Log the search in this project — use the Spanish-orthography spelling (with the diacritic) rather than an Anglicised form, since FTS does not transliterate.

judge_context: Should recognise that pre-1850 Latin American notarial protocolos are exactly the thin-index source class that the skill's references/search-strategies.md flags as FTS-preferred — and should explain the rationale briefly to the user
Should construct a query whose keywords include the Spanish-orthography 'García' (with the diacritic, or at minimum the unaccented 'Garcia') — NOT an Anglicised form like 'Garcia/Garcis/Garsey'
Should successfully recover the fixture's 1834 poder otorgado (recordPlace: Habana, recordType: Notarial), which records García as the apoderado on a power of attorney

Should NOT mistakenly treat the prompt as out-of-scope and refuse — Latin American notarial coverage is explicitly listed in the skill's allowed scope (references/transcription-quirks.md 'Latin American notarial protocols (17th–19th c.)' under Strong coverage)
Should mention in passing that FTS searches the AI transcript and Spanish-orthography variants (e.g. accented vs unaccented) may need separate queries if the canonical query returns nothing
